title: Top-Down Prior Overrides Weak Evidence

runtime.expected_outcome: When mu_2 starts far from y and pi_2 is high, mu_1 ends up closer to mu_2 than to y -- the predictive-coding analog of perceptual illusion.

audiences.kid: If you're expecting one thing and the world whispers another, you might still 'see' what you expected.
audiences.real: At equilibrium, mu_1 is the precision-weighted average of y and mu_2. Strong priors can override noisy evidence.
audiences.equation: mu_1* = (pi_1 y + pi_2 mu_2)/(pi_1 + pi_2). When pi_2 >> pi_1, mu_1* ~ mu_2.
audiences.derivation: Set gradient = 0 in the Gaussian-PC update: pi_1 (y - mu_1) - pi_2 (mu_1 - mu_2) = 0 -> mu_1 = (pi_1 y + pi_2 mu_2)/(pi_1 + pi_2). Precision-weighted average (Bogacz 2017).

orchestrate.objective: Exhibit how a strong top-down prior overrides noisy bottom-up evidence.
orchestrate.context: y=1.0, mu_2=-1.0, pi_1 in {0.5, 2.0}, pi_2 in {0.5, 5.0}; record equilibrium mu_1.

credits: Parr, Pezzulo & Friston (2022), Active Inference, MIT Press (CC BY-NC-ND), Ch 5.
Clark (2013), Whatever next? Predictive brains, situated agents.
